item.title: 🎨 Beginner-Friendly Guide 'Number of Ways to Paint N 3 Grid' – LeetCode 1411 (C++, Python, JavaScript)
item.author: Om Shree
item.content:encoded: Combinatorics and dynamic programming can often feel like solving a massive puzzle with missing pieces. This problem is a classic example of how observing small patterns can help us derive a mathematical formula to solve much larger problems efficiently.A grid of size (n rows and 3 columns).Three available colors: Red, Yellow, and Green.A rule that no two adjacent cells (horizontal or vertical) can have the same color.Calculate the total number of ways to paint the entire grid.Return the result modulo .To solve this, we focus on the patterns within a single row. Because there are only 3 columns, we can categorize every valid row into two types:ABA Pattern (2 colors used): The first and third cells are the same color, and the middle cell is different (e.g., Red-Yellow-Red). There are 6 such combinations.ABC Pattern (3 colors used): All three cells have different colors (e.g., Red-Yellow-Green). There are also 6 such combinations.The magic happens when we move from row to row . We calculate how many new ABA and ABC patterns can be placed below an existing one without violating the adjacency rule.Below an row, you can fit 3 new ABA-style rows and 2 new ABC-style rows.Below an row, you can fit 2 new ABA-style rows and 2 new ABC-style rows.By maintaining these two counts and updating them for each new row, we avoid complex recursion and solve the problem in linear time. Instead of tracking every single color combination, we grouped them into patterns (ABA and ABC), which simplified the logic significantly. The solution runs in time because we only iterate through the number of rows once. When dealing with counts that grow exponentially, using the modulo operator at each step prevents integer overflow.This problem is a fantastic introduction to State-Transition Dynamic Programming. In real-world software engineering, these types of logic patterns are used in and . If you were designing a scheduling system where no two consecutive tasks can use the same specialized hardware, the underlying logic would look very similar to this. Mastering these "counting" problems is a huge step toward passing technical interviews at top-tier firms.

item.title: How to Compile and Install Python 3.12+ on Amazon Linux 2
item.author: bhaktraj
item.content:encoded:
Amazon Linux 2 does not provide Python 3.12 via default repositories. To use the latest Python features, better performance, and modern TLS security, we must compile Python from source.Builds OpenSSL 1.1.1 manuallyCompiles Python 3.12 against itInstalls Python without breaking system PythonCreates a virtual environment safely🔹 Script Overview – What This Script Does✔ Updates the system ✔ Installs development tools ✔ Builds OpenSSL from source ✔ Sets environment variables for secure compilation ✔ Builds Python 3.12 from source ✔ Installs Python safely using altinstall ✔ Creates a Python virtual environmentyum update yum groupinstall yum openssl-devel bzip2-devel libffi-devel zlib-devel readline-devel sqlite-devel ncurses-devel gdbm-devel db4-devel expat-devel
yum gcc gcc-c++ make perl-core zlib-devel /tmp/ wget https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-1.1.1w.tar.gz xzf openssl-1.1.1w.tar.gz openssl-1.1.1w ./config /usr/local/openssl /usr/local/openssl shared zlib make make | /etc/ld.so.conf.d/openssl.conf ldconfig yum xz-devel libuuid-devel tk-devel tcl-devel openssl version /tmp/ wget https://www.python.org/ftp/python/3.12.0/Python-3.12.0.tgz xzf Python-3.12.0.tgz Python-3.12.0
./configure /usr/local make make altinstall
make altinstall /usr/local/bin/python3.12 /usr/local/bin/pip3.12
/usr/local/bin/python3.12 venv venv venv/bin/activate ✅ Recommended & SAFE Symlink (Best Practice)🔹 Create symlink for python3.12 /usr/local/bin/python3.12 /usr/bin/python3.12
🔹 Create symlink for pip3.12 /usr/local/bin/pip3.12 /usr/bin/pip3.12
python3.12 pip3.12

item.title: FHIR Integration: Build Modern Healthcare Apps Using Python and FastAPI
item.author: wellallyTech
item.content:encoded:
Healthcare data has historically been locked inside fragmented, proprietary systems. For developers, getting these different platforms to communicate has been a significant hurdle.FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is the modern standard designed to solve this. It leverages familiar web technologies to make data sharing more accessible than ever before. If you are ready to start building, this guide covers the essential foundation for compliant data exchange.
Why FHIR is the New Standard Previous healthcare standards were often rigid and difficult to implement. FHIR changes the landscape by focusing on developer experience and web-first principles.It operates using , which are essentially "Lego blocks" for medical data. Whether you are dealing with a , an , or a , each resource is clearly defined and consistent.By using an , FHIR suggests a more flexible way to connect apps. It relies on standard RESTful actions like and , making it intuitive for anyone familiar with modern web development.
Building a FHIR Facade with Python Building a "FHIR Facade" allows you to bridge the gap between legacy systems and modern applications. This layer exposes data through a compliant API without requiring a complete database overhaul.To get started, we recommend using and the library. This combination offers high performance and automatic data validation using Pydantic models.Key development steps include:: Initializing a Python virtual environment and installing .: Using Pydantic to ensure all incoming JSON matches FHIR specifications.: Assigning unique IDs to resources like the object for easy retrieval.
Production-Ready Checklist Building a basic server is the first step, but production environments require additional rigor. Use the following checklist to ensure your implementation is secure and scalable.Protects sensitive patient data in transit.Prevents non-compliant data from entering the system.Tells other systems what your API is capable of doing.Moves beyond in-memory storage to PostgreSQL or SQL Server.
Moving Toward Interoperability Mastering FHIR is no longer optional for developers in the health-tech space. It provides the framework needed to build connected, patient-centric applications that actually work together.By implementing the and interactions correctly, you ensure your application speaks the universal language of healthcare. This consistency is associated with fewer integration errors and faster deployment cycles.Ready to see the code and build your own server? Read the for a complete technical walkthrough and implementation details.

item.title: **Title:** Market Momentum Shift: Why Stocks Must Regain Footing to Avoid Bearish Trends
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Market Momentum Shift: Why Stocks Must Regain Footing to Avoid Bearish Trends "As bullish technical indicators falter, investors are left wondering if stocks can regain momentum and avoid a bearish market downturn. In this article, we'll delve into the current market trends and explore the key factors that could influence the direction of the stock market."The stock market has been experiencing a rollercoaster ride in recent months, with investors struggling to find a clear direction. Despite the initial optimism, bullish technical indicators have begun to break down, raising concerns about a potential bearish market trend. As investors, it's essential to understand the current market dynamics and the factors that could influence the direction of the stock market.Bullish Technical Indicators Under PressureBullish technical indicators, such as moving averages and relative strength index (RSI), have been a crucial tool for investors to gauge market sentiment. However, in recent weeks, these indicators have started to break down, signaling a potential shift in market momentum. The breakdown of these indicators has led to increased volatility, making it challenging for investors to make informed decisions.Key Factors Influencing Market TrendsSeveral key factors are influencing the direction of the stock market, including:: The release of economic indicators, such as GDP growth, inflation rates, and employment numbers, can significantly impact market trends. A strong economy can boost investor confidence, while a weak economy can lead to a bearish market trend.: Global events, such as trade wars, geopolitical tensions, and natural disasters, can also impact market trends. These events can create uncertainty, leading to increased volatility and a potential bearish market trend.: Central bank policies, such as interest rate decisions and quantitative easing, can also influence market trends. A change in monetary policy can impact investor sentiment and market direction.: Corporate earnings reports can also impact market trends. A strong earnings season can boost investor confidence, while a weak earnings season can lead to a bearish market trend.What's Next for the Stock Market?As the stock market continues to navigate the current market dynamics, investors must remain vigilant and adapt to changing market conditions. To avoid a bearish market trend, stocks must regain footing and demonstrate a clear direction. Investors can take several steps to navigate the current market:: Diversifying your portfolio can help reduce risk and increase potential returns.: Stay informed about market trends, economic indicators, and global events to make informed investment decisions.Monitor Technical Indicators: Monitor technical indicators, such as moving averages and RSI, to gauge market sentiment and potential market trends.Adjust Your Investment Strategy: Adjust your investment strategy to reflect changing market conditions and investor sentiment.The stock market is experiencing a critical juncture, with bullish technical indicators breaking down and a potential bearish market trend looming. To avoid a bearish market trend, stocks must regain footing and demonstrate a clear direction. By understanding the current market dynamics and key factors influencing market trends, investors can make informed investment decisions and navigate the current market. Remember to stay informed, diversify your portfolio, and adjust your investment strategy to reflect changing market conditions.

item.title: **Title:** Essential Tech Upgrades for Your First Apartment: Boosting Convenience and Safety
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Essential Tech Upgrades for Your First Apartment: Boosting Convenience and Safety Moving into your first apartment is an exciting milestone, but it can also be a daunting experience, especially when it comes to setting up your new space. With the right gadgets, you can transform your apartment into a smart, convenient, and safe haven. In this article, we'll explore the top 10 must-have tech upgrades that will make your life easier and more enjoyable.Section 1: Smart Home SecurityWhen it comes to home security, you want to ensure that your apartment is protected from potential threats. Here are a few essential gadgets to consider:: Upgrade your traditional door lock to a smart lock that can be controlled remotely using your smartphone. This feature allows you to grant access to guests or service providers, and receive notifications when someone enters or exits your apartment.: Invest in a comprehensive home security system that includes cameras, motion sensors, and alarm systems. These systems can be monitored remotely, providing you with peace of mind and an added layer of protection.Section 2: Safety and ConvenienceIn addition to home security, there are several other gadgets that can enhance your safety and convenience:: Install smart smoke detectors that can detect potential fires and alert you and your neighbors in case of an emergency.: Wake up to a gentle sunrise simulation that helps regulate your circadian rhythms and starts your day on a positive note.: Control the temperature in your apartment with a smart thermostat that learns your schedule and preferences to optimize energy efficiency.Section 3: Smart Home AutomationTake your smart home experience to the next level with these innovative gadgets:: Integrate a voice assistant, such as Amazon Alexa or Google Assistant, into your smart home setup to control various devices with voice commands.: Install smart light bulbs that can be controlled remotely and adjust their color temperature and brightness to create a cozy atmosphere.: Use smart plugs to turn any device into a smart device, allowing you to control and monitor its energy usage.Upgrading your first apartment with these essential tech gadgets can significantly enhance your living experience. From smart home security to safety and convenience features, these innovations can help you create a comfortable, convenient, and secure space that meets your needs and preferences. Whether you're a tech-savvy individual or just starting to explore the world of smart home technology, these gadgets are sure to make a positive impact on your daily life.

item.title: **Title:** India Cracks Down on X's AI-Powered Chatbot, Grok, Over Obscene Content Concerns
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: India Cracks Down on X's AI-Powered Chatbot, Grok, Over Obscene Content Concerns In a recent move, India's Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) has issued a stern warning to Elon Musk's X (formerly Twitter) to rectify its AI-powered chatbot, Grok, within a 72-hour timeframe. The directive stems from concerns over the chatbot's propensity to generate obscene and objectionable content, which has raised eyebrows among Indian authorities.Grok is an AI-powered chatbot developed by X, designed to engage users in conversations and provide information on various topics. While the chatbot's primary intention is to assist and educate users, its ability to generate content has raised concerns about its potential misuse. The chatbot's AI algorithms can sometimes produce responses that are deemed explicit or objectionable, sparking concerns about its impact on users, particularly minors.India's IT Ministry Takes ActionIn response to the concerns, India's IT Ministry has issued a formal notice to X, directing the company to submit an action-taken report within 72 hours. The report is expected to outline the measures X will take to rectify the issue and prevent the chatbot from generating obscene content in the future. The ministry's move is a clear indication of its commitment to ensuring that online platforms adhere to Indian laws and regulations.Implications for X and the AI IndustryThe Indian government's move has significant implications for X and the broader AI industry. As AI-powered chatbots become increasingly prevalent, concerns about their potential misuse are growing. The incident highlights the need for companies to develop and implement robust content moderation policies and AI algorithms that can detect and prevent the generation of explicit or objectionable content.The Indian government's directive to X to rectify its AI-powered chatbot, Grok, is a significant development in the ongoing debate about the regulation of AI-powered chatbots. As the AI industry continues to evolve, it is essential for companies to prioritize content moderation and ensure that their AI algorithms are designed to prevent the generation of explicit or objectionable content. The incident serves as a reminder of the importance of responsible AI development and the need for companies to adhere to regulatory requirements.Recommendations for X and the AI IndustryTo address the concerns raised by the Indian government, X and other companies in the AI industry should consider the following recommendations:Develop robust content moderation policies: Companies should establish clear policies for content moderation and ensure that their AI algorithms are designed to detect and prevent the generation of explicit or objectionable content.Implement AI algorithms that prioritize user safety: Companies should develop AI algorithms that prioritize user safety and well-being, and ensure that their chatbots are designed to provide accurate and helpful information.Engage with regulatory bodies: Companies should engage with regulatory bodies and stakeholders to ensure that their AI-powered chatbots comply with relevant laws and regulations.By following these recommendations, X and other companies in the AI industry can ensure that their AI-powered chatbots are designed to provide value to users while minimizing the risk of explicit or objectionable content.

item.title: Day 39 of improving my Data Science skills
item.author: Sylvester Promise
item.content:encoded: A silent struggle in data work that frustrates a lot of people (and no one talks about it): "I'm learning a lot… but everything feels disconnected."Today reminded me that the problem isn't learning too little, it's not seeing how the pieces fit while you're learning. So here's how my today went, 4+ hours deep into data, and honestly, it was beautiful. I started with Introduction to Data Science in Python. The course frames learning as solving mysteries with data, which made even the basics interesting again: importing and using modules creating variables setting up the foundation for analysisNothing brand new here, but context made it click differently.Then I continued Introduction to Importing Data in Python I picked up from yesterday and worked more with relational databases: querying databases in Python learning that any SELECT statement can be ordered by any column using ORDER BYThese are small SQL detail but big impact when working with real systems.I moved into data visualization, this time with Seaborn Here I learned: how to create scatterplots how to use count plots how Seaborn works seamlessly with pandas DataFramesIt reinforced something important: visualization is more about asking better questions of your data rather than just displaying charts.I started Introduction to Functions in Python, and I looked at, functions with and without parametersAgain, not all new, but clearer.Under Python Toolbox, I learned about Iterators This part surprised me: the difference between iterables and iterators how the next() method actually works and yes, file connections are iterables tooThat explained a lot of "magic" I'd previously taken for granted.Finally, Cleaning Data in Python This might have been my favorite part today: handling complete vs incomplete duplicates deciding when to: drop duplicates or use groupby with meaningful summary statisticsThis is the part of data work that quietly determines whether insights are trusted or ignored.What today taught me? Some things weren't new. Some things were. But everything connected. And that's the part people struggle with: Learning tools is easy. Learning how they fit together takes intention. I'm continuing the year doing exactly that, learning deeply, documenting honestly, and respecting the unglamorous parts of data work. And yeah, I completed another chapter today !Happy New Year once again🎉Here's to cleaner data, clearer thinking, and fewer "why doesn't this make sense?" moments in 2026.

item.title: Building a Resilient Edge Architecture for Remote Farms with Starlink + LoRa
item.author: Daniel Azevedo Novais
item.content:encoded: The Problem: Connectivity in "Off-the-Grid" Agriculture Imagine managing a 5,000-hectare soybean farm in rural Argentina. You need: : Soil moisture, temperature, pest detection: Automated harvest readiness analysis: Irrigation control, pesticide drones: <100ms for actuator commands* Fiber optic doesn't reach there. Traditional satellite (GEO) has 600ms+ latency. 4G coverage is spotty. So I built a to validate a hybrid architecture combining:✅ (LEO satellite - 20-40ms latency)✅ (automatic failover)✅ (low-power sensors)✅ (local processing + ML inference)✅ (QoS, VLAN segmentation, SD-WAN)

item.title: Why Your AI Agent Needs a Passport
item.author: Ramprasad G
item.content:encoded:
The security gap that's about to become a crisis
The Problem No One's Talking About Your LangChain agent can read your emails. Your AutoGPT can execute code. Your AI assistant can access your bank's API.But how does your server know it's really YOUR agent making that request?Right now, the answer is: it doesn't.Most AI agents authenticate using:This worked when agents were toys. It won't work when they're managing your infrastructure.Agent A: "Transfer $10,000 to account 12345" Server: "Who are you?" Agent A: "I'm... an agent?" Server: "Whose agent? Can you prove it?"Now imagine a malicious agent impersonating yours:Evil Agent: "Transfer $10,000 to MY account" Server: "Who are you?" Evil Agent: "I'm definitely the authorized agent, trust me bro" Server: "Seems legit ✓"This is not hypothetical. As agentic AI scales, this attack vector becomes inevitable.When a human logs into your app, they prove their identity with:Something they know (password)Something they have (phone/hardware key)Something they are (biometrics)When an AI agent calls your API, it should prove: it intends to do (intent) it was authorized (timestamp) you should trust it (reputation)This is exactly what does.Vouch is conceptually simple:# Agent side: Sign your intent
from vouch import Signer signer = Signer(private_key=PRIVATE_KEY, did="did:web:myagent.com") token = signer.sign({ "action": "transfer_funds", "amount": 100, "to": "account_123" })
# Send token with API request response = requests.post(API_URL, headers={"Vouch-Token": token})
# Server side: Verify identity + intent
from vouch import Verifier valid, passport = Verifier.verify(token, public_key) if valid: print(f"Agent: {passport.iss}") # did:web:myagent.com print(f"Intent: {passport.payload}") # {"action": "transfer_funds", ...} print(f"Reputation: {passport.reputation_score}") # 85/100
That's it. Cryptographic proof of identity + intent in 10 lines of code.
Why Not Just Use JWT/OAuth? Good question. Here's the difference: ✅ Designed for: Humans ❌ Identity model: Centralized (Google, Auth0) ❌ Intent signing: No ❌ Non-repudiation: No ❌ Agent-to-agent: Awkward ✅ Designed for: AI Agents ✅ Identity model: Decentralized (DID) ✅ Intent signing: Yes ✅ Non-repudiation: Cryptographic proof "Who is this user?" "Who is this agent, what do they want to do, and should I trust them?"Just as the internet needed HTTPS, the agentic web needs cryptographic identity.┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Your Application │ ├─────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Vouch Protocol (Identity) │ ← We're building this ├─────────────────────────────────────┤ │ LangChain / CrewAI / AutoGen │ ├─────────────────────────────────────┤ │ LLM (GPT-4, Claude) │ └─────────────────────────────────────┘ Without this layer, every agent framework is building on sand.pip install vouch-protocol from vouch import generate_identity, Signer, Verifier # Generate agent identity identity = generate_identity(domain="myagent.com") print(f"Your DID: {identity.did}")
# Sign an action signer = Signer(identity.private_key_jwk, identity.did) token = signer.sign({"action": "hello_world"})
# Verify it valid, passport = Verifier.verify(token, identity.public_key_jwk) print(f"Valid: {valid}, Agent: {passport.iss}") Vouch Protocol is open source (Apache 2.0) and actively developed:🔐 Ed25519 cryptographic signatures🎭 Decentralized identity (DIDs)🔗 Integrations for LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, MCP✅ 107 tests, OpenSSF badge in progressThe Open Standard for AI Agent Identity & AccountabilityWhen Anthropic launched MCP, they solved "how agents call tools." They didn't solve "how we TRUST those agents."Vouch Protocol is the SSL certificate for AI agents.AI agents are making real-world API calls with of:Healthcare AI accesses patient data → HIPAA violation riskFinancial AI makes unauthorized trades → Liability nightmareCustomer service AI leaks data → Compliance failure → No agent-specific features, security mistakes easy → Most people just YOLO it and hope for the best provides cryptographic identity for AI agents, modeled after SSL/TLS:✅ (industry-standard cryptography) ✅ (works with existing…The question isn't if AI agents will need cryptographic identity. It's whether you'll build it yourself, wait for a breach to force the issue, or adopt a standard now.Vouch Protocol is that standard.

item.title: Why Your Celery Dashboard is Lying to You (and How I’m Using AI to Fix It)
item.author: Hernan Chilabert
item.content:encoded: We've all been there. The Datadog dashboard is a sea of green. Redis is connected. Workers are "Online." Yet, the support tickets are flooding in: "Why haven't I received my password reset email?"I realized that standard monitoring has a massive blind spot. It monitors infrastructure status, not business health.The Problem: The "Silent Killers" In high-scale environments, your system doesn't always "crash." It degrades silently.: Processes that are alive in Docker but disconnected from the Celery control plane.: Tasks that drop out of Redis without a single log entry.: A short queue that hides a task stuck for 45 minutes because of a misconfigured visibility timeout.An Audit CLI with a "Brain" I decided to build a tool that automates the manual troubleshooting I used to do via SSH. But listing metrics wasn't enough. I wanted a report that a Senior Dev could act on in seconds.I integrated Claude to act as a "Virtual SRE." Instead of just showing you a configuration value, the CLI interprets the data.The Report: Check out this output from a recent test:Your visibility_timeout is 30 minutes. I found 3 tasks that regularly exceed 45 minutes.💀 Impact if uncaught: These tasks WILL execute twice. Both will complete → duplicate execution.🎯 Business Impact: If duplicates, you'll have duplicate inventory entries. If duplicates, you'll email customers twice.⚡ Fix (add to celeryconfig.py): broker_transport_options = {'visibility_timeout': 7200} # 2 hoursLogs are for machines; reports are for humans. By using LLMs to cross-reference task history with infrastructure settings, the CLI doesn't just find bugs; it finds . It explains the , the , and the .I'm currently stress-testing this on production environments. If you're tired of "green dashboards" that hide real problems, I'd love for you to join the beta.Which are your 'silent killers'? How did you fix them?

item.title: 2025 Was the Year of Fast AI, 2026 Will Be the Year of AI Quality
item.author: Sospeter Mong'are
item.content:encoded:
The AI conversation in 2025 was dominated by one word: .Faster models. Faster responses. Faster releases.Companies raced to ship AI features before competitors did. Developers optimized for latency. Startups marketed “instant” intelligence. The goal was simple: make AI respond faster than humans can think.And it worked up to a point.But as we move into 2026, a clear shift is happening. Speed alone is no longer impressive. Quality is becoming the real differentiator.2026 will be the year AI grows up.In 2025, the AI ecosystem was in an arms race:Model providers competed on response times and token throughputProducts rushed AI features into production to avoid being left behindUsers were impressed by how quickly AI could generate text, code, images, and answersSpeed was visible. It was easy to demo. It was easy to sell.However, speed also exposed weaknesses:Confident but wrong answersHallucinations dressed as certaintyGeneric outputs that looked good but lacked depthFast AI wasn’t always AI.
The Problem Speed Couldn’t Solve As AI adoption deepened, expectations changed.Can this model , not just respond?Can it follow context, constraints, and intent?Can it work reliably in ?Speed alone couldn’t answer these questions.A fast mistake is still a mistake. A fast hallucination is still a liability.This is where 2026 begins.
The Shift to AI Quality in 2026
1. From Fast Responses → Correct & Grounded Answers In 2026, accuracy will matter more than immediacy.Slower but verifiable answersAI that cites sources and shows reasoningOutputs that are grounded in facts, data, or systems of recordQuality AI will know when it doesn’t know—and say so.
2. From General Intelligence → Domain Mastery 2025 celebrated “AI that can do everything.” 2026 will reward AI that does one thing exceptionally well.Healthcare-focused assistantsLegal, education, and developer-specialized AIEnterprise AI trained on internal knowledge bases
3. From Prompting Skills → System Design In 2025, knowing how to “prompt well” was a skill.In 2026, value shifts to:Combining retrieval, memory, tools, and validationBuilding guardrails and evaluation systemsTreating AI as part of software architecture, not a magic boxAI quality will come from , not clever prompts.
4. From Raw Output → Actionable Intelligence Fast content generation is no longer enough.Understand goals and constraintsProduce outputs that lead to decisions and actionsIntegrate directly into workflows (CRMs, ERPs, codebases, APIs)Reduce human correction, not increase itThe question shifts from “How fast did it respond?” to
5. From Experimentation → Accountability In 2025, “AI is experimental” was an acceptable excuse.Businesses will demand reliabilityErrors will have consequencesAI outputs will need auditing, logging, and traceabilityTrust and safety will be competitive advantagesAI will be judged like any other production system.
What This Means for Builders, Developers, and Leaders Focus less on flashy demos, more on robustnessInvest in evaluation, testing, and feedback loopsOptimize for user trust, not just engagementUnderstanding data, systems, and context will matter more than model choiceAI engineering will look more like backend engineering than prompt writingAI strategy will shift from “adoption” to “impact”The winners will be those who align AI with real business problems2025 proved that AI can be fast. 2026 will prove whether AI can be .Speed got AI into the room. Quality will determine whether it stays.The next wave of AI winners won’t be those who respond first—but those who respond .

item.title: 125 Inspirational Good Morning Messages for Positive Energy
item.author: Good Morning Quotes
item.content:encoded: Starting your day with the right mindset can completely transform how you feel, think, and act. “125 Inspirational Good Morning Messages for Positive Energy” is a carefully curated collection designed to help you begin each morning with optimism, motivation, and inner strength. Whether you’re waking up to a busy workday, personal challenges, or new opportunities, the right words can reset your mood and guide your energy in a positive direction.These inspirational good morning messages focus on encouragement, gratitude, self-belief, and hope. They remind you that every sunrise brings a fresh start and a chance to move forward with confidence. From short, uplifting lines perfect for WhatsApp status updates to deeper messages you can reflect on during your morning routine, this collection offers something for everyone.Sharing these messages with friends, family, or colleagues is a simple yet powerful way to spread positivity and strengthen relationships. A thoughtful good morning message can brighten someone’s day, boost their motivation, and help them feel supported before the day begins.Whether you use them for personal inspiration, social media posts, or daily affirmations, these 125 inspirational good morning messages are meant to fill your mornings with positive energy, clarity, and purpose—helping you start each day on a hopeful and empowered note.

item.title: Postbear: The Postman alternative in your terminal
item.author: carban
item.content:encoded:
Meet : The Postman alternative your terminal has been waiting for!Hey everyone! I love Postman, but sometimes I just want to stay in my terminal, keep things lightweight, and move fast. I couldn't find a TUI/CLI tool that hit that "sweet spot" of power and simplicity—so I built Postbear as alternative.Postbear is a high-speed, lightweight API client designed for developers who live in the command line. No heavy Electron apps, just simplicity.
Why you’ll love Postbear: ⚡ Fast: Starts instantly. No splash screens, no loading bars. 🪶 Minimalist: Small footprint, ready to go. 🛠️ Powerful: Handles headers, body data, parameters, and support to .http files. 💻 Terminal-First: Built for the modern dev workflow. All the examples here uses fooapi.com an API created by me some months ago. The platform provides realistic dummy data across several categories, which you can use to mock your projects and ideas. Here is the first post I made, check it out, it is awseome! FooAPI First Postpostbear .http filepath] postbear run method] endpoint]
🌟 I need your help to grow! Postbear is open-source and just getting started. I have big dreams for this tool, and I’d love for the community to help shape its future. Star it on GitHub: It helps others find the project! Try it out: Give it a spin and let me know what you think. Contribute: This is a personal project any feedback is welcome. for major changes, please open an issue first to discuss what you would like to change.

item.title: Package a django app
item.author: Erlan Akbaraliev
item.content:encoded:
What's a package, project structure
1. 📦 What is a Python Package? A package is simply a directory of Python modules (.py files). To be recognized by Python, it typically contains an file.The core philosophy of packaging is reusability:you can also use other peoples packages and integrate them into your own projectI have a poll application with preset questions. On this webapp you can choose options for a given preset question and see statistics after submitting your choice.. ├── core │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── __pycache__ │ ├── asgi.py │ ├── settings.py │ ├── urls.py │ └── wsgi.py ├── db.sqlite3 ├── manage.py └── polls ├── __init__.py ├── __pycache__ ├── admin.py ├── apps.py ├── migrations ├── models.py ├── static ├── templates ├── test_models.py ├── test_views.py ├── urls.py └── views.py Every feature related to poll application is done inside the app 'polls' app. Let's turn the polls app into a package and upload it to a website of packages.Feel free to create a simple django project with a single view that returns HttpResponse('Hello Package!') or similar and follow along.Create another directory separate from the project. You can call it , this will be the name of our package.Move app of your project to directory and rename to Edit so that name refers to the new module name 'django_polls' and add labels to give a short name to the app 'polls' 5.Create file with the following content:============ django-polls ============
django-polls is a Django app to conduct web-based polls. For each question, visitors can choose between a fixed number of answers.
Detailed documentation is in the "docs" directory.
Quick start -----------
1. Add "polls" to your INSTALLED_APPS setting like this::
INSTALLED_APPS = [ ..., "django_polls", ]
2. Include the polls URLconf in your project urls.py like this::
path("polls/", include("django_polls.urls")),
3. Run ``python manage.py migrate`` to create the models.
4. Start the development server and visit the admin to create a poll.
5. Visit the ``/polls/`` URL to participate in the poll. Create fileCreate django-polls/pyproject.toml file which details how to build and install the app. It must have the following content: You can check django version using this command:8.Create with the following content. It's used to templates and static files:recursive-include django_polls/static * recursive-include django_polls/templates * 9.Build your package by running:It creates dist/ directory containing Source Distribution (.tar.gz) and your Built Distribution (the .whl or "wheel" file). I personally refer to them as zipped version of my app.
3. Installing the django-polls package Since we moved polls directory out of the project, it's no longer working. We can fix our project by installing the package.python3 pip django-polls/dist/django_polls-0.1.tar.gz 2.Update of your projectINSTALLED_APPS = [ "django_polls.apps.PollsConfig", ..., ] 3.Update to point to the new module nameurlpatterns = [ path("polls/", include("django_polls.urls")), ..., ] 4.Run the development server to confirm the project is continues to workNow if you run , you should see 'django-polls' package.python3 -m twine upload --repository testpypi dist/* During the process it will ask you for API token, copy, paste the token you just created.After the command completes, you should see something like this:Uploading distributions to https://test.pypi.org/legacy/ Enter your API token: Uploading example_package_YOUR_USERNAME_HERE-0.0.1-py3-none-any.whl 100% ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 8.2/8.2 kB • 00:01 • ? Uploading example_package_YOUR_USERNAME_HERE-0.0.1.tar.gz 100% ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 6.8/6.8 kB • 00:00 • ? Now if you want to install the version of the package that you just uploaded to test.pypiUninstall the previously locally installed django-polls pip uninstall django-polls pip https://test.pypi.org/simple/ django-polls-erlan Replace 'django-polls-erlan' with the package name you gave when building. You can also access the install command by clicking on 'View'.

item.title: Day 3 of 100
item.author: Palak Hirave
item.content:encoded: Today I learned how to use if/else as well as conditional operators. I also learned about the modulo operation, len() function and logical operators. After using some examples and making a few of my own, I got the hang of this and went on to learning about nested if statements and elif statements. I also praciced using multiple if statements in succession and noting the difference between multiple if statements and using elif multiple times. And to finish off, I made a pizza order game and a BMI calculator that also gauges if a person is under/over weight or normal using averages that I found online.

item.title: **Production-Ready Go Docker Containers: Small, Secure, and Efficient Containerization Guide**
item.author: Nithin Bharadwaj
item.content:encoded:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world! Let’s talk about putting a Go application into a Docker container that’s ready for production. I don’t mean just any container—I mean a small, secure, and efficient one. Over the years, I've learned that a bloated container can slow you down, increase costs, and introduce security risks. So, let me walk you through how I build them.The goal is simple: package your application so it runs consistently anywhere, using as little space and memory as possible, without cutting corners on security. We'll start with a basic Go web application. Here’s what that might look like.This app has three endpoints: a root handler, a health check, and a simple metrics endpoint. It's a typical starting point. The real work begins with the Dockerfile. This is where many people go wrong by creating a single, bulky stage that includes the compiler, source code, and the final binary all in one image.Instead, I use a method called a multi-stage build. Think of it like building a car in a factory and then shipping only the finished car, not the entire factory. The first stage is the builder stage, where we compile the Go code.apk add git ca-certificates tzdata adduser appuser
go mod download
0 linux amd64 go build git describe +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ /app . Notice a few things. I start with , which is a smaller base image. I install only what’s needed: for version info, for SSL, and for timezone support. I also create a non-root user called right here in the builder stage. This is a security practice I always follow; the application should not run as root.The build flags are crucial. creates a static binary that doesn’t depend on C libraries from the operating system. The strips debug symbols, making the binary smaller. The flags inject version and build time directly into the binary, which is helpful for tracking what’s running.Now, here’s the magic of multi-stage. I can create a second stage that starts from a completely empty base, called . CMD ["/app/app", "health"]
TZ=UTC
The image is literally empty. It contains no operating system, no shell, nothing. This is the ultimate minimal footprint. Into this empty container, I copy only what the static binary needs: the SSL certificates, timezone data, the user information file, and the binary itself. That’s it.The result is an image that’s often just 5 to 10 megabytes. It starts in milliseconds. I once replaced a 1.2 GB development-style image with a 7 MB one like this, and the performance and security improvements were immediate.But a small image isn’t useful if it’s insecure. Let’s talk about security hardening. I already mentioned the non-root user. Running as means if someone finds a way into the container, they have very limited privileges. I also copy the file just to define that user; the container won’t even have a shell for them to use.Another layer is a security scan. I can add a dedicated scanning stage to my Dockerfile using a tool like Trivy.trivy filesystem HIGH,CRITICAL /app This stage scans the compiled binary for known vulnerabilities before it ever gets into the final image. It acts as a gate in the build process. In a team setting, this can be enforced in your continuous integration pipeline.Now, development and testing are just as important. I use Docker Compose to create a consistent local environment that mirrors the build stage.This file lets me run the application in development with hot-reloading (), run my test suite, and even run linters, all within isolated containers that share a cached module directory. It keeps my local machine clean and ensures everyone on the team has the same experience.Automation is key for consistency. I write a simple shell script to handle the build, test, scan, and push process.git describe docker build ::latest builder Dockerfile docker run /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock aquasec/trivy:latest image HIGH,CRITICAL : docker run : go ./...
docker build /:/:latest Dockerfile This script ensures every production image is tagged with a version, passes a security scan, and passes all tests. It turns a complex series of commands into a single, reliable step.Let’s not forget about the application’s behavior inside the container. The instruction in the Dockerfile is vital. It tells the container platform (like Docker Swarm or Kubernetes) how to check if my app is alive and ready. My endpoint returns a simple JSON status. Without this, your orchestrator won’t know if your app is stuck starting up or has crashed.Resource limits are another practical consideration. While not in the Dockerfile itself, when you run or deploy the container, you should set limits. A Go application’s garbage collector can be tuned, but it works better if it knows its constraints. If you tell Kubernetes the container needs 100Mi of memory, the Go runtime can manage its heap more efficiently.Configuration should always come from outside the container. My app reads the from an environment variable. For secrets like API keys, I use the secret management system built into my orchestrator, never baking them into the image or passing them via build arguments.Monitoring is built right into the sample application with the endpoint. It exposes Go runtime metrics in a format that Prometheus can scrape. When this container runs in Kubernetes, I can set up a ServiceMonitor to collect these metrics automatically. All application logs go to standard output (), where they can be collected by tools like Fluentd or Loki.Finally, let’s validate that our container works as expected with some integration tests.These tests can be run inside the container during the build process to ensure the packaged application responds correctly.To sum it up, building a production-ready container is about intentional choices. Use multi-stage builds to separate the build environment from the runtime environment. Start from or a minimal base like . Always run as a non-root user. Inject version information at build time. Set up health checks and expose metrics. Automate security scanning and testing.
The outcome is a container that is small, fast, and secure. It uses resources predictably and fits perfectly into modern, automated deployment pipelines. It gives you confidence that what runs on your laptop will run the same way in the cloud, with no surprises. This approach has served me well across countless projects, and it’s a foundation you can build upon for any Go service. 📘 , , , and to the channel! is an AI-driven publishing company co-founded by author . By leveraging advanced AI technology, we keep our publishing costs incredibly low—some books are priced as low as —making quality knowledge accessible to everyone.Stay tuned for updates and exciting news. item.title: LLMs - Integrating with Chatbots - Complete Tutorial
item.author: Hemanath Kumar J
item.content:encoded:
In the evolving landscape of artificial intelligence, Large Language Models (LLMs) have carved a niche for themselves, especially in enhancing the capabilities of chatbots. This tutorial is designed for intermediate developers looking to integrate LLMs with chatbots to create more dynamic, responsive, and intelligent systems. Through this guide, you'll grasp the practical steps needed to achieve this integration, supported by relevant code examples.Basic understanding of chatbot development.Familiarity with a programming language (preferably Python).Access to a Large Language Model API (like OpenAI's GPT).
Step 1: Setting Up Your Environment Before diving into the integration, ensure your development environment is set up correctly. This includes installing the necessary libraries and tools, such as for API calls in Python.
Step 2: API Authentication Authenticate your application with the LLM's API provider. This typically involves generating an API key and including it in your API requests.
Step 3: Sending Requests to the LLM Create a function to send prompts to the LLM and receive responses. This function will be the core of your chatbot's intelligence.
Step 4: Integrating LLM Responses with Your Chatbot Now, integrate the LLM responses into your chatbot's workflow. This might involve parsing the response and formatting it appropriately for your chatbot's UI.To better understand the integration process, here are additional code examples illustrating more complex interactions and customizations.Maintain context in a conversation by storing and sending previous exchanges as part of the prompt.Customize the LLM's responses based on your chatbot's personality or specific use cases.: Secure your API keys and ensure encrypted communication with the LLM.: Optimize the performance by caching frequent requests or responses.: Tailor the LLM's responses to fit the tone and context of your chatbot.Integrating LLMs with chatbots opens up a myriad of possibilities for creating more engaging, intelligent, and responsive applications. By following this tutorial, developers can leverage the power of LLMs to enhance their chatbot projects, ensuring a more sophisticated user experience.

item.title: Pixel-Powered Encryption: Turning an Image into a Key with Python
item.author: Shayan Ijaz
item.content:encoded: > Ever wondered if an image could be your secret key? Not a password, not a string of numbers—an actual picture. Each pixel contributes to your encryption key, and without the exact same image, your message is unreadable.That’s exactly what I explored in a Python prototype, and today I’ll show you how it works and how you can try it yourself.Images are basically data. Each pixel has red, green, and blue values (RGB). If we take these values and combine them in a predictable way, they can serve as a key stream for encryption. PPM format (P3) is perfect for this experiment: it’s a plain-text image format, easy to parse. Each pixel = three numbers (R, G, B) between 0 and 255. The order of pixels matters—mess with one pixel, and decryption fails. Think of it like this: your message is a lock, and your image is the key. Only the exact key image can open it.We use a simple XOR encryption:Convert your message to bytes.Convert the image pixels into a keystream. For each pixel, we collapse RGB into a single byte: 3. key_byte = (R + G + B) % 256 XOR each message byte with the corresponding key byte.Decryption is the same process—XOR is symmetric. : This is not secure encryption for real secrets. It’s a concept experiment for learning and fun.: You don’t need advanced crypto knowledge to see how it works.: The key is literally an image.: You can modify pixels, shuffle, layer multiple images, etc. Even though it’s not secure, it’s a great playground for:Learning about image file formats (PPM)Understanding basic encryption conceptsExploring creative coding ideasTry it or Contribute on : https://github.com/urshayan/PpmCrypt

item.title: Build Scalable Real-Time WebSocket Server in Go: Complete Guide with Code Examples
item.author: Nithin Bharadwaj
item.content:encoded:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world! Let's talk about building a chat system, a live dashboard, or a multiplayer game. The common thread is instant updates. When you click send, you expect everyone else to see the message right away. That's the world of real-time communication, and for that, we often use WebSockets.Think of a traditional website like a series of letters. You send a request (a letter), and you wait for a response (a return letter). It's slow. A WebSocket is like a phone call. Once connected, both you and the server can talk and listen at any time, instantly. My job is to manage thousands of these simultaneous "phone calls" efficiently. That's what we're going to build.We'll use Go because it's brilliant at handling many things at once. Its lightweight goroutines are perfect for managing many connections without drowning in complexity. Let's start from the ground up.The heart of our system is the . It's the switchboard operator. Its job is to accept incoming calls, keep track of who is connected, and route messages to the right people.When a client first connects, it's just a regular HTTP request. We need to "upgrade" it to a WebSocket connection. This function does that handshake.Once the connection is upgraded, we create a . This object represents that single, ongoing phone call with a user. It holds the actual connection, a unique ID, and some crucial mechanics: a channel for sending messages and a channel for knowing when to hang up.Why a channel for sending? This is a key pattern. We separate reading and writing into two independent loops, or "pumps." The listens for messages coming the client. The listens for messages that need to be sent the client.This separation is vital. It prevents a slow-reading client from blocking us from sending messages to them, and vice-versa. They operate on separate tracks.Here's the . It sits in a loop, waiting for one of three things: a message to send, a timer to send a "ping," or a signal that the connection is closed.The is simpler. It constantly listens. When a message arrives, it processes it. If it gets an error or a close message, it triggers the to tell the to finish.Now, where do we put all these objects? We can't just leave them lying around. We need a directory. That's the .The pool has two main maps. One maps a Client ID to the Client object. The other is for groups: which clients are in "room-5" or "notifications"? The (Read-Write Mutex) is the bouncer at the door. It allows many goroutines to the map at the same time (to send a message), but only one goroutine to (to add or remove a client). This keeps things fast and safe.Adding and removing clients is straightforward with the pool.Notice . This is how we politely tell the goroutine to stop. When we close a channel, any goroutine receiving from it gets a signal. Our sees become and shuts itself down. No leaks.Let's send a message. The simplest is a broadcast: shout to everyone.We use because we're only reading the map. The with a case is a non-blocking send. If the client's channel buffer is full (maybe their network is slow), we don't wait. We just move on. This prevents one slow client from holding up a message for everyone else.But broadcasting to everyone is wasteful. What if you're in a chat room? You only need messages for that room. That's where groups come in. First, a client joins a group.Now, sending to a group is efficient. We look up the group, get the list of member IDs, and only send to them.Sometimes connections die without saying goodbye. The network drops. A user closes their laptop. We can't wait forever. This is where heartbeats come in. Remember the in the ? It sends a Ping message periodically.The sets up a "Pong handler." When it receives a Pong (the automatic response to a Ping), it updates the connection's time.We can then have a cleanup goroutine that runs every minute, checking for connections that haven't been active for, say, two ping intervals. Those are stale and can be removed.What about more complex routing? Imagine a stock ticker. Thousands of clients subscribe to "GOOG" or "TSLA." A pub/sub (publish/subscribe) is perfect for this. It's more dynamic than pre-defined groups.A client subscribes to a channel:When new data arrives for "TSLA," we publish it. The hub looks up all subscribers for that channel and places a broadcast message into a central channel. Another worker goroutine would read from this channel and actually send the messages out via the . This decouples receiving an event from the work of distributing it.We also need to know how our server is doing. Are we handling the load? We use atomic counters for that. They're fast and safe for concurrent use.Finally, no single server can handle everything. We need to plan for scale. A can sit in front of multiple WebSocket server instances. It directs new connections to different servers, often in a simple round-robin fashion.If one server fails, the load balancer stops sending new connections to it. Existing clients on that server would try to reconnect, and the load balancer would point them to a healthy one. Your application needs to be designed to handle this reconnection gracefully, perhaps by temporarily storing state in a shared database like Redis.Putting it all together, starting the server looks like this:Building this is like constructing a careful, well-organized city. The is the directory of all phone lines. The and are the dedicated operators for each line. The is the specialized broadcasting station. The mutexes are the traffic rules. The heartbeat and cleanup are the maintenance crew.
You start with a simple connection. Then you add management so you don't lose track. Then you add efficient routing so you don't shout in empty rooms. Then you add monitoring and resilience. Each piece addresses a real problem you'll hit as more and more users connect. The goal is a system that stays responsive and stable, whether there are ten connections or ten thousand. 📘 , , , and to the channel! is an AI-driven publishing company co-founded by author . By leveraging advanced AI technology, we keep our publishing costs incredibly low—some books are priced as low as —making quality knowledge accessible to everyone.Stay tuned for updates and exciting news.
